# ARC-QUIC-RFC9001-0003 - QUIC RFC 9001 Repeated 1-RTT Key Update Lifecycle Boundary Architecture

## Purpose

Define the design boundary for widening the current first-successor 1-RTT Key Phase support into a general RFC 9001 key-update lifecycle without weakening existing packet-protection behavior.

## Design Summary

The existing implementation is intentionally a bounded lifecycle floor plus a helper-backed AEAD key-use floor: after handshake confirmation it can derive and install the first 0-to-1 1-RTT successor material, retain the displaced old open/protect material in a dedicated connection-owned lifecycle object, retain the first next receive material alongside the current receive material for apparent peer updates, detect the changed Key Phase bit, retry opening a first observed phase-1 packet with successor material only when the opened packet carries the next Key Phase bit, drop invalid Key Phase probes without close/datagram signaling, keep the optional unconfirmed consecutive peer-update policy timing-neutral by dropping apparent pre-confirmation consecutive updates without closing, update send keys before emitting ACKs for peer-updated packets, avoid sending first peer-update ACKs with retained old protect material, allow KEY_UPDATE_ERROR for retained old-key ACK packets that acknowledge tracked first-successor phase-1 sends, keep first-update higher packet numbers protected with same or newer material, use retained old receive material for lower recovered packet numbers once the first phase-1 packet-number floor is known, use next receive material for higher recovered packet numbers in the first successor transition, reject first old-key packets that violate that packet-number ordering with KEY_UPDATE_ERROR, arm a first retained-old discard deadline for three PTOs after authenticating a new-key packet, discard that retained old first-update material together with matching sender and recovery state when the deadline expires, clear the retained next receive material when that successor is installed, record the first current-phase acknowledgment, enforce the first three-PTO post-confirmation local-update cooldown, install a bounded local phase-2 update after acknowledgment/cooldown/retained-old discard, authenticate lower recovered phase-1 packets after local phase-2 install, accept a peer phase-2 second-successor packet only after the current phase is acknowledged and prior retained-old material is discarded, clean up retained phase-1 old keys with matching sender/recovery state after local or peer phase-2 packets arm the retention timer, install a bounded local phase-3 update after phase-2 acknowledgment/cooldown/retained-old discard, open bounded peer phase-3 packets after phase-2 confirmation, use retained phase-2 keys for phase-3 lower recovered packet numbers, apply old-key ACK KEY_UPDATE_ERROR policy to phase-2 ACK packets that acknowledge phase-3 sends, clean up retained phase-2 old keys with matching sender/recovery state after phase-3 packets arm the retention timer, install a bounded local phase-4 update after phase-3 acknowledgment/cooldown/retained-old discard, open bounded peer phase-4 packets after phase-3 confirmation, send ACKs with phase-4 keys, use retained phase-3 and phase-4 next keys for repeated packet-number selection, apply old-key ACK KEY_UPDATE_ERROR policy to phase-3 ACK packets that acknowledge phase-4 sends, clean up retained phase-3 old keys with matching sender/recovery state after phase-4 packets arm the retention timer, install a bounded local phase-5 update after phase-4 acknowledgment/cooldown/retained-old discard, open bounded peer phase-5 packets after phase-4 confirmation, send ACKs with phase-5 keys, use retained phase-4 and phase-5 next keys for repeated packet-number selection, apply old-key ACK KEY_UPDATE_ERROR policy to phase-4 ACK packets that acknowledge phase-5 sends, and clean up retained phase-4 old keys with matching sender/recovery state after phase-5 packets arm the retention timer. Representative later-phase fuzz/property coverage now exercises the same repeated local-update gate, peer next-key opening, old-key ACK KEY_UPDATE_ERROR parity, and retained-previous cleanup shape across phases 6 through 24 to guard against hard-coded phase-5 behavior. The current runtime wires helper-backed AEAD-limit decisions into bounded 1-RTT send/open paths: the first confidentiality-limit send can install the first key update, exhausted successor protection stops and discards the connection, and integrity-limit receive paths discard without emitting close datagrams. This wiring now proves a bounded repeated AEAD-limit-triggered local update after the current phase is acknowledged, the repeated cooldown has elapsed, and retained old material has been discarded, plus endpoint helper-backed stateless-reset emission and automatic retained-route host reset response after AEAD-limit terminal discard when an existing token, retained route, matching remote address and port, loop-prevention state, and rate gates allow it; it still does not claim broader stateless-reset response behavior or a complete unbounded epoch ledger. TLS KeyUpdate prohibition stays separate from QUIC Key Phase updates, and the current first-update CRT slice remains supporting evidence rather than proof of complete repeated lifecycle support.

## Data and State Considerations

The implementation now has a dedicated connection-owned 1-RTT key-update lifecycle object for retained next receive material, retained old first-update packet-protection material, the first retained-old discard deadline, current-phase acknowledgment state, and the first repeated local-update cooldown deadline rather than widening the TLS bridge's first-successor fields directly. The sender and recovery ledgers now tag tracked 1-RTT packets with the retained old key phase so old epochs can be discarded without dropping current packet-protection material or current-phase acknowledgment/cooldown state. A reusable AEAD key lifecycle helper now tracks protected/opened packet counters per key lifecycle, stops protection when the configured confidentiality limit is reached, and classifies exhausted AEAD usage into key-update, stop-connection, or stateless-reset-only actions. The endpoint retains registered stateless-reset tokens, token-linked connection ID routes, and path bindings after AEAD-limit terminal discard so direct callers can request reset-only emission and host receive loops can map a later packet destination connection ID to a retained token only when the remote address and port still match. Representative phases 6 through 24 coverage now exercises the generic helper shape, and retained-old expiry now preserves current-phase acknowledgment/cooldown ownership across sampled later epochs; the future ledger expansion must still add epoch identifiers, repeated current/next/old open and protect material ownership as a product/runtime model, current outbound Key Phase, packet-number ranges observed per phase, broader stateless-reset response behavior outside the proven AEAD-limit retained-route floor, and unbounded old-read-key retention/discard ownership beyond representative retained-previous cleanup checks. The TLS bridge and key schedule remain responsible for deriving material from retained traffic secrets and for surfacing prohibited TLS KeyUpdate violations.

## Edge Cases and Constraints

- The current supported runtime floor remains a first 0-to-1 successor install with retained next receive material, retained old 1-RTT material, first peer-initiated updated-key ACK protection, first authenticated retained-old discard timer plus discard cleanup, bounded phase-2 install and receive selection, bounded local phase-3 install plus retained phase-2 cleanup, bounded phase-4 local/peer opening and retained phase-3 cleanup, bounded phase-5 local/peer opening and retained phase-4 cleanup, representative phases 6 through 24 property/fuzz coverage for the repeated lifecycle shape, helper-backed AEAD counter/stop-use and AEAD-limit policy classifier state, and bounded runtime AEAD update-or-discard wiring only.
- Only bounded local and peer phase-2 install paths plus their retained phase-1 cleanup, bounded local phase-3 install plus retained phase-2 cleanup, bounded phase-4 local/peer opening plus retained phase-3 cleanup, bounded phase-5 local/peer opening plus retained phase-4 cleanup, and representative later-phase shape checks are implemented, and only after current-phase acknowledgment plus prior retained-old discard; endpoint helper-backed stateless-reset emission and automatic retained-route host reset response after AEAD-limit terminal discard are proven, but broader stateless-reset response behavior remains unimplemented until that response boundary is traced and verified.
- Authentication failure while trying successor material must not advance the active key epoch.
- Duplicate same-phase packets must not retrigger derivation or discard retained old material.
- Only the first-successor old/current/next receive-selection, old-key packet-number ordering, and retained-old discard floors are implemented; broader old/current/next epoch selection remains unimplemented until the lifecycle ledger exists.
- Repeated old 1-RTT key discard must stay synchronized with packet number, reordering, retention-window, AEAD-usage, and sender/recovery state, and it must not reset current-phase acknowledgment or cooldown state that belongs to the current epoch.
- Runtime AEAD-limit wiring is limited to first-update/repeated-update-or-discard plus bounded repeated local update after the existing current-phase acknowledgment/cooldown/old-key-discard gate, endpoint helper-backed reset emission after terminal discard, and automatic retained-route host reset response after terminal discard; broader stateless-reset response behavior remains blocked until its lifecycle requirements and proof plan are traced.
- TLS KeyUpdate messages remain prohibited and are not a substitute for QUIC Key Phase updates.

## Alternatives Considered

- Treat the current first-successor install path as general key-update support. Rejected because the bridge state currently permits only the first 0-to-1 install and has no epoch-retention or repeated-toggle model.
- Compare only Key Phase bits and derive successor material opportunistically on every observed toggle. Rejected because the receiver needs authentication, rollback, duplicate, and old-key retention rules before accepting later epochs.
- Fold old 1-RTT key discard into the RFC 9002 early-key discard helper. Rejected because 1-RTT key-update discard is tied to application-data epochs, reordering, and sender/recovery ownership, not just Initial, Handshake, or rejected 0-RTT cleanup.

## Risks

- If repeated key updates reuse the first-update bridge fields without an epoch ledger, the receiver can lose the ability to authenticate packets protected with retained old keys.
- If old 1-RTT key discard is implemented without sender/recovery ownership, bytes-in-flight and retransmission state can outlive the usable packet-protection epoch.
- If AEAD usage-limit counters trigger local updates without the repeated-update acknowledgment gate, the implementation can violate the current-phase acknowledgment requirement or advance into an unrecoverable limit state.

## Open Questions

- Broader stateless-reset response behavior outside the bounded AEAD-limit retained-route floor still needs explicit requirements, packet-to-token lookup boundaries, lifecycle design, and proof before implementation.

## Current Boundary

This artifact records a stabilized lifecycle boundary, not a complete repeated-update implementation claim. The current executable proof covers the first-successor install, retained next first-update receive material, retained old first-update 1-RTT material, timing-neutral invalid Key Phase drop classification, timing-neutral no-close handling for apparent unconfirmed consecutive peer updates, the first three-PTO retained-old discard timer after authenticating a new-key packet, first old-key discard with matching sender/recovery cleanup, first peer-initiated updated-key packet opening and ACK-key update, bounded peer phase-2 second-successor packet opening and ACK-key update after current-phase confirmation plus old-key discard, bounded local phase-3 install after phase-2 acknowledgment/cooldown/old-key discard, bounded peer phase-3 packet opening, phase-3 retained phase-2 lower-recovered receive selection, phase-2 old-key ACK KEY_UPDATE_ERROR parity, retained phase-2 timer arming and cleanup, bounded local phase-4 install after phase-3 acknowledgment/cooldown/old-key discard, bounded peer phase-4 packet opening and ACK-key update after phase-3 confirmation, phase-4 retained phase-3 lower-recovered and phase-4 higher-recovered selection, phase-3 old-key ACK KEY_UPDATE_ERROR parity, retained phase-3 timer arming and cleanup, bounded local phase-5 install after phase-4 acknowledgment/cooldown/old-key discard, bounded peer phase-5 packet opening and ACK-key update after phase-4 confirmation, phase-5 retained phase-4 lower-recovered and phase-5 higher-recovered selection, phase-4 old-key ACK KEY_UPDATE_ERROR parity, retained phase-4 timer arming and cleanup, the first KEY_UPDATE_ERROR floor for retained old-key ACK packets that acknowledge first-successor phase-1 sends, first-update send-key ordering, the first old/current next receive-selection floors for lower and higher recovered packet numbers, bounded phase-2 lower and higher recovered receive-selection floors, the first KEY_UPDATE_ERROR floor for old-key packet-number ordering violations, helper-backed per-key-set protected/opened-packet counting, confidentiality-limit stop-use, AEAD-limit policy classification, bounded runtime AEAD first-update/repeated-update/discard wiring, endpoint helper-backed stateless-reset emission after AEAD-limit terminal discard, automatic retained-route host reset response after AEAD-limit terminal discard, and Key Phase observation coverage under the RFC 9001 Section 6 requirement homes plus the CRT `REQ-QUIC-CRT-0145` slice.

## Traffic Secret Advancement Boundary

The runtime now commits key updates by replacing the stored client and server application traffic secrets with their updated `quic ku` successors. This lets helper derivation compute the next successor packet-protection material from the advanced secrets for bounded local and peer phase-2 install paths, the bounded local/peer phase-3 install paths, the bounded local/peer phase-4 topoff paths, the bounded local/peer phase-5 topoff paths, and representative later phases 6 through 24. This is not a complete epoch ledger; the unbounded repeated-epoch cleanup follow-on is tracked separately under `9001-06-repeated-epoch-ledger-ownership`.

## Bounded Repeated Local Install Boundary

The runtime now exposes a narrow repeated local 1-RTT install seam after the first current phase is acknowledged, the three-PTO cooldown has elapsed, and retained-old first-update material has been discarded. The seam derives the next `quic ku` successor from the advanced traffic secrets, retains the displaced phase-1 open/protect material, increments the internal key-phase counter to phase 2, and emits outbound Key Phase using phase parity so phase 2 is signaled with bit 0. This is a send-side/local-install floor only; it does not claim repeated receive epoch selection, second-successor packet acceptance, peer-initiated repeated updates, broader stateless-reset-only response behavior.

## Bounded Repeated Lower-Recovered Receive Boundary

After a bounded local phase-2 install, the receive path now treats retained old material as the immediately previous key phase by parity rather than as a hard-coded phase-0 epoch. The packet-number floor used to reject old-key packets is tracked together with the current key-phase counter, so stale phase-1 observations do not poison phase-2 lower-recovered selection. This proves lower recovered phase-1 packets can still be authenticated with retained previous material after local phase 2 becomes current, while retained phase-1 packets that carry the phase-0 bit remain rejected. It does not accept peer-initiated second-successor packets before local phase-2 install and does not implement peer-initiated repeated updates.

## Bounded Repeated Peer Second-Successor Boundary

After the current phase is acknowledged and prior retained-old material has been discarded, the receive path can retain second-successor open material, authenticate a peer phase-2 packet with the next keys and Key Phase bit 0, install corresponding phase-2 open/protect material, retain displaced phase-1 material as old, and emit ACKs with phase-2 send keys. Apparent second-successor packets before current-phase confirmation remain timing-neutral drops. This does not claim broader stateless-reset-only behavior.

## Bounded Repeated Old-Key Discard Boundary

After local or peer phase-2 install, retained phase-1 material uses the same three-PTO retention timer and expiration path as the first successor floor, but the retained key-phase identifier is phase 1 instead of phase 0. After bounded local phase-3 install, retained phase-2 material uses the same timer and cleanup path with retained key-phase identifier 2. After the bounded phase-4 topoff, retained phase-3 material uses the same timer and cleanup path with retained key-phase identifier 3. After the bounded phase-5 topoff, retained phase-4 material uses the same timer and cleanup path with retained key-phase identifier 4. Representative later-phase property coverage exercises the same retained-previous cleanup shape across phases 6 through 24. Expiration discards retained old read material and removes matching old-phase sender/recovery state while leaving current packet-protection material and current-phase acknowledgment/cooldown state intact. Sampled later-phase proof now guards that cleanup isolation across phases beyond the previous representative range. This remains helper-backed proof; the unbounded repeated epoch-ledger cleanup model is tracked separately under the dedicated follow-on.

## Lifecycle Ledger Shape

- The current executable floor already arms one retained-old discard deadline from the first authenticated successor packet and clears matching old-phase sender/recovery state when it expires.
- The current AEAD lifecycle helper counts protected/opened packets per key lifecycle, rejects further protection once the configured confidentiality limit is reached, and classifies exhausted AEAD usage into key-update, stop-connection, or stateless-reset-only actions.
- The endpoint helper can emit a stateless reset after AEAD-limit terminal discard only when the caller supplies the retained handle/sequence and existing token, loop-prevention, and rate gates allow it.
- The connected endpoint host and listener host can emit a retained-route stateless reset after AEAD-limit terminal discard only when packet parsing recovers a token-linked retained connection ID route for the same remote address and port, and the same loop-prevention and rate gates allow it.
- The future ledger keeps the current epoch, optionally precomputed next receive epoch, and retained old receive epoch distinct.
- Local initiation is allowed only after handshake confirmation and only after the current phase has produced an acknowledged packet.
- Peer initiation installs corresponding send keys before acknowledging the new-key packet.
- Packet-number ordering decides whether retained previous, current, or next receive material is eligible for opening a packet.
- The current runtime applies AEAD-limit policy decisions to bounded first-update/repeated-update-or-discard send/open paths; bounded repeated AEAD-limit local requests use the existing repeated-update gate; broader stateless-reset response behavior still needs its own trace.
- Repeated old read-key discard must remove the corresponding secrets and sender/recovery state together.

## Next Planned Scope

- Keep broader stateless-reset response behavior outside the bounded AEAD-limit retained-route floor blocked until packet-to-token lookup, response eligibility, lifecycle, loop prevention, and rate gating requirements are traced.
- The dedicated `9001-06-repeated-epoch-ledger-ownership` follow-on now owns the remaining repeated epoch-ledger cleanup work beyond the representative later-phase proof.
